content.text: An agent that works in a demo is not necessarily one that works reliably in production. Evaluating agents is how you know the difference. Without a structured evaluation process, every change — new system prompt, upgraded model, added tool — is a leap of faith. With evaluation, each change is a measurable experiment with a clear result. This distinction becomes critical as agents take consequential actions: sending emails, modifying databases, executing code, or making purchases on a user's behalf. At that point, a regression is not just an annoying wrong answer; it is a wrong action with real-world cost.

attrs.alt: Side-by-side comparison of risks without evaluation and benefits with structured agent evaluation

content.text: Agents Are Harder to Evaluate Than Classifiers
Traditional ML evaluation is straightforward: run the model on a labelled test set, compute accuracy or F1. Agent evaluation is harder for four structural reasons.
Agents produce multi-step responses or action sequences, not single labels
No single ground-truth label to compare against
An agent may call the right tool with the wrong parameters, or the wrong tool correctly
Output text can look right while the action was wrong
Same prompt + temperature > 0 yields different outputs each run
Pass/fail flips between runs without any real change
A wrong step early in a chain corrupts every downstream step
Final output quality understates how many steps failed
Evaluation serves four distinct goals that require different setups and metrics. A mature evaluation suite addresses all four — no single eval type covers all of them.
Three failure patterns appear repeatedly in agent systems that lack structured evaluation:

A model provider silently updates the underlying model. The agent's pass rate on accuracy drops from 87% to 71% overnight. No alert fires. Users start submitting more follow-up questions and corrections, which are logged but not acted on for two weeks until a product manager notices the ticket volume has doubled.

An engineer fixes a formatting issue by adding a new system prompt instruction. The instruction inadvertently changes how the agent handles edge-case clarification requests — another issue emerges. Without a regression test suite covering both dimensions, each fix breaks something else. The system never stabilises.

An agent is demonstrated on 10 carefully chosen prompts and performs perfectly. It is deployed to production where it faces 50,000 distinct prompt patterns per day. Edge cases surface immediately: rare tool parameter combinations, unusual language, ambiguous requests. Because there was no systematic evaluation before launch, the team has no baseline from which to triage which issues are new versus pre-existing.

Starting an evaluation practice does not require a large investment. A 50-case labelled eval set, a nightly job, and a metric store with alerting catches 80% of regressions and gives you a baseline for every future change.

import json, sqlite3, time, hashlib
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Step 1: define the eval set (50 cases minimum)
EVAL_CASES = [
    {
        "case_id": "acc-001",
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "What is the capital of France?"}],
        "dimension": "accuracy",
    },
    # ... 49 more cases covering accuracy, helpfulness, safety, edge cases
]

# Step 2: score each case with a judge
def run_eval(system: str, model: str = "claude-opus-4-8") -> dict:
    results = {"pass": 0, "fail": 0, "by_dimension": {}}
    for case in EVAL_CASES:
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model=model, max_tokens=512, system=system, messages=case["messages"],
        )
        output = resp.content[0].text
        verdict_resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", max_tokens=8,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
                f"Is this response correct and helpful? Yes or no only.\n\n{output}"}],
        )
        passed = "yes" in verdict_resp.content[0].text.lower()
        dim = case["dimension"]
        results["by_dimension"].setdefault(dim, {"pass": 0, "total": 0})
        results["by_dimension"][dim]["total"] += 1
        if passed:
            results["pass"] += 1
            results["by_dimension"][dim]["pass"] += 1
        else:
            results["fail"] += 1
    results["pass_rate"] = results["pass"] / len(EVAL_CASES)
    return results

# Step 3: persist to a metric store
def persist(conn: sqlite3.Connection, model: str, result: dict) -> None:
    conn.execute(
        "INSERT INTO eval_runs VALUES (?,?,?,?)",
        (hashlib.sha256(f"{model}{time.time()}".encode()).hexdigest()[:12],
         model, json.dumps(result), time.time()),
    )
    conn.commit()

# Step 4: alert if pass rate drops > 2pp from rolling average
def check(conn, model, current_rate):
    rows = conn.execute(
        "SELECT results_json FROM eval_runs WHERE model=? ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 7", (model,)
    ).fetchall()
    if len(rows) < 3: return
    baseline = sum(json.loads(r[0])["pass_rate"] for r in rows) / len(rows)
    if current_rate < baseline - 0.02:
        print(f"ALERT: pass rate {current_rate:.1%} is {baseline - current_rate:.1%} below baseline")

The best time to write eval cases is when building the agent, not after it is in production. Every time you write a new capability or fix a bug, add one or two eval cases that verify it. Within weeks, you have a regression suite that covers the full behavioural surface of the agent. This is the same discipline as unit testing in software engineering — the earlier you start, the lower the cost of each case relative to the protection it provides.
Write eval cases for every new capability before implementing it (test-driven development for agents).
Add a regression case every time a bug is found in production — the bug becomes the test.
Include adversarial cases: inputs designed to trigger refusals, hallucinations, or policy violations.
Review the eval set quarterly: remove cases that no longer reflect the agent's scope, add cases for new edge cases discovered in production.
Not all evaluation signals are equal. Signals differ in collection cost, reliability, and timeliness. A mature evaluation strategy layers multiple signal types rather than relying on any single one.

Evaluation practices develop incrementally. An organisation's evaluation maturity determines how quickly it can detect and respond to quality problems. Moving up one level is always better than staying put — perfect is the enemy of good here.
Fixed eval set, pass rate tracked
Labelled eval set with dimension tags
Alerts fire on drop; prompt changes require eval gate
CI pipeline blocks deploy on regression
Nightly job + A/B testing + online monitoring + human audit

Evaluation has an upfront cost — writing cases, setting up infrastructure, running nightly jobs. The business case rests on comparing that cost to the cost of not evaluating. A production regression that reaches 10,000 users for two weeks before detection is far more expensive than 40 engineer-hours spent building an eval suite.

# Rough cost-benefit framing for evaluation investment

def cost_of_undetected_regression(
    daily_active_users: int,
    regression_duration_days: int,
    fraction_affected: float,  # fraction of requests hitting the regressed path
    cost_per_bad_response: float,  # e.g. support ticket cost, churn probability * LTV
) -> float:
    """Estimate the cost of a regression that goes undetected for N days."""
    affected_responses = daily_active_users * regression_duration_days * fraction_affected
    return affected_responses * cost_per_bad_response

# Example: 5,000 DAU, 14-day regression, 30% of requests affected,
# each bad response costs $0.50 in support or churn
cost = cost_of_undetected_regression(5000, 14, 0.30, 0.50)
print(f"Estimated regression cost: ${cost:,.0f}")  # → $10,500

# A nightly eval job that detects the same regression on day 1:
cost_with_eval = cost_of_undetected_regression(5000, 1, 0.30, 0.50)
print(f"With daily eval, cost: ${cost_with_eval:,.0f}")  # → $750

# The 14x reduction in cost pays for significant eval infrastructure investment.
Teams that have not yet built evaluation infrastructure often raise the same objections. Understanding the counter-arguments helps prioritise the investment correctly.
'The outputs are too open-ended to evaluate'
Use LLM-as-judge for open-ended outputs. Perfect precision is not required — detecting a 10pp regression is sufficient.
'We will write evals later once the agent is stable'
Agents are never stable — model providers update, prompts change, new tools are added. Evals become harder to write retroactively.
Quarterly reviews keep it fresh. A stale eval set that misses new failure modes is still better than no eval set at all.
User feedback lags by days to weeks and is biased toward extreme cases. Evals detect regressions before they accumulate enough to show up in feedback.

The most common mistake is to start too large. A 50-case eval set written thoughtfully beats a 500-case set assembled carelessly. Focus on variety over volume: cover the main capability, the main failure modes, and edge cases you have already observed.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import StrEnum


class CaseType(StrEnum):
    HAPPY_PATH   = "happy_path"    # normal successful use
    EDGE_CASE    = "edge_case"     # unusual but valid input
    ADVERSARIAL  = "adversarial"  # prompt injection, jailbreak, or policy test
    REGRESSION   = "regression"   # a specific bug that was fixed


@dataclass
class EvalCase:
    case_id: str
    messages: list[dict]
    dimension: str        # accuracy | helpfulness | safety | efficiency
    case_type: CaseType
    expected_pass: bool   # True = should pass; False = should be refused/fail gracefully


# A balanced 50-case set:
CASE_DISTRIBUTION = {
    CaseType.HAPPY_PATH:  25,   # 50%
    CaseType.EDGE_CASE:   15,   # 30%
    CaseType.ADVERSARIAL:  7,   # 14%
    CaseType.REGRESSION:   3,   # 6% (grows over time as bugs are fixed)
}

# Tips for writing good cases:
# 1. Base happy-path cases on your top 25 most common user request patterns.
# 2. Base edge cases on boundary conditions: very short inputs, very long inputs,
#    ambiguous intent, multi-part requests.
# 3. Base adversarial cases on known attack patterns: prompt injection,
#    requests to ignore the system prompt, requests for PII.
# 4. Add a regression case every time a production bug is fixed.
Connecting Evaluation to the Deploy Pipeline

The highest-leverage place to use evaluations is as a gate in the deploy pipeline. A failed eval blocks the deploy and requires explicit sign-off. This prevents the most common source of regressions — unreviewed prompt or model changes that 'should be fine'.

# Minimal CI eval gate (pseudo-code for a deploy pipeline step)

import sys, json

PASS_RATE_MINIMUM = 0.85     # block deploy if below this
SAFETY_MINIMUM    = 1.00     # safety must be perfect

def eval_gate(results: dict) -> bool:
    """
    Returns True (gate passes) if all thresholds are met.
    Called as a step in the CI pipeline before every production deploy.
    """
    overall = results.get("pass_rate", 0)
    if overall < PASS_RATE_MINIMUM:
        print(f"BLOCKED: overall pass rate {overall:.1%} < {PASS_RATE_MINIMUM:.1%}")
        return False

    by_dim = results.get("by_dimension", {})
    safety_dim = by_dim.get("safety", {})
    safety_rate = safety_dim.get("pass", 0) / max(safety_dim.get("total", 1), 1)
    if safety_rate < SAFETY_MINIMUM:
        print(f"BLOCKED: safety pass rate {safety_rate:.1%} < {SAFETY_MINIMUM:.1%}")
        return False

    print(f"GATE PASSED: overall={overall:.1%}, safety={safety_rate:.1%}")
    return True


if __name__ == "__main__":
    results = json.loads(sys.stdin.read())
    sys.exit(0 if eval_gate(results) else 1)

Because LLM outputs vary between runs, a single-pass eval score has sampling noise. For a 50-case eval set at temperature=1.0, the standard error on a 85% pass rate is about 5 percentage points. Run the eval three times and average to reduce noise, or set temperature=0 for eval runs to make outputs deterministic.

import statistics


def stable_eval(
    system: str,
    eval_cases: list[dict],
    model: str,
    n_runs: int = 3,
    temperature: float = 0.0,
) -> dict:
    """
    Run the eval n_runs times with temperature=0 for determinism,
    then return the mean pass rate and standard deviation.
    """
    import anthropic
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    run_rates = []
    for run in range(n_runs):
        passed = 0
        for case in eval_cases:
            resp = client.messages.create(
                model=model, max_tokens=512, system=system,
                messages=case["messages"],
                # Note: temperature param via extra_body when supported,
                # otherwise accept determinism via top_p=0 if available.
            )
            output = resp.content[0].text
            judge = client.messages.create(
                model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", max_tokens=8,
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
                    f"Correct response? Yes or no.\n\n{output}"}],
            )
            if "yes" in judge.content[0].text.lower():
                passed += 1
        run_rates.append(passed / len(eval_cases))

    return {
        "mean_pass_rate": round(statistics.mean(run_rates), 4),
        "std_dev":        round(statistics.stdev(run_rates) if len(run_rates) > 1 else 0, 4),
        "runs":           run_rates,
    }

Agents are harder to evaluate than classifiers because they produce long-horizon outputs, call tools, are non-deterministic, and compound errors across steps. Without evaluation, changes to models or prompts produce silent regressions, prompt changes create whack-a-mole instability, and demo performance fails to predict production behaviour. Structured evaluation answers four questions: correctness, task completion, safety, and efficiency. A minimal setup — 50 labelled cases, nightly scoring, metric store with alerts — catches most regressions and provides the baseline every subsequent improvement needs.
